title: Gender
examTip: Don't just say 'women earn less' — specify the type of inequality (horizontal segregation, vertical segregation, glass ceiling) and name a sociologist or statistic to back it up.
introTips: The gender pay gap is real but complicated — it changes depending on whether you include part-time work.
There are different feminist explanations for gender inequality — liberal, radical, Marxist and Black feminist views all give different answers.
Intersectionality is a high-value concept for top-mark answers — it shows you understand that 'women' is not one uniform group.
intro: If you've ever been told certain jobs 'aren't for girls,' or noticed that most CEOs look similar, you've already spotted something sociologists have been studying for decades. Gender shapes who earns what, who leads what, who does the housework and who gets heard in politics — and the explanations for why this happens are fiercely contested.

keyPoints.heading: Gender Inequality in Work and Pay
keyPoints.content: The gender pay gap refers to the difference between men's and women's average hourly earnings. In the UK, the full-time median hourly gap was 7.7% in 2023 (ONS), rising to 14.9% when part-time workers are included — because women are far more likely to work part-time. Over a working life, women earn on average £300,000 less than men. Two types of occupational segregation explain much of this. Horizontal segregation means men and women cluster in different types of jobs: nursing is 89% female, construction is 87% male. Vertical segregation (the glass ceiling) means that even in mixed industries, women are underrepresented at the top: only 33% of FTSE 100 directors were female in 2022, up from just 5% in 2011. The sticky floor describes women being stuck in the lowest-paid tiers of organisations, while the glass escalator (Williams, 1992) describes how men in female-dominated professions (nursing, teaching) rise faster than their female colleagues.
writeBullets: Gender pay gap — 7.7% full-time; 14.9% including part-time (ONS, 2023)
Lifetime earnings gap — women earn ~£300,000 less over a working life
Horizontal segregation — men and women in different job types (nursing 89% female; construction 87% male)
Vertical segregation / glass ceiling — women underrepresented in senior roles (33% FTSE 100 directors, 2022)
Sticky floor — women trapped in lowest-paid tiers of organisations
Glass escalator (Williams, 1992) — men in female-dominated jobs rise faster than women

keyTerms.def: The difference between men's and women's average hourly earnings — 7.7% full-time, 14.9% including part-time (ONS, 2023)
Men and women concentrated in different types of jobs
Women underrepresented at senior levels even within the same industry
The invisible barrier that prevents women from reaching the top of organisations
Williams (1992) — men in female-dominated professions rise faster than women in the same field

keyPoints.memTip: Horizontal = sideways (different jobs); Vertical = upward (blocked from the top). Glass ceiling blocks going up; sticky floor traps you at the bottom.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often muddle horizontal and vertical segregation — use a diagram with arrows if needed.

keyPoints.heading: Explaining Gender Inequality
keyPoints.content: Sociologists offer several competing explanations for gender inequality in work and pay. Human capital theory (neoclassical economics) suggests women invest less in education or careers because they anticipate career breaks for childcare — but this is undermined by the fact that women now outperform men in education at every level. Radical feminist theory blames patriarchy: systematic male dominance that operates through male networks, masculine workplace cultures and outright discrimination. Sylvia Walby (1994) argued patriarchy operates across six structures: paid work, housework, sexuality, culture, violence and the state. Marxist feminist theory links gender inequality to capitalism: women's unpaid domestic labour reproduces the workforce for free, and women act as a 'reserve army of labour' (Braverman) — drawn into the workforce when needed, pushed out when not. Liberal feminist theory emphasises legal reform — the Sex Discrimination Act 1975 and Equality Act 2010 have improved things, but gaps persist. Structurally, the UK has some of the highest childcare costs in the OECD — women disproportionately cut hours after having children, creating a 'motherhood penalty' versus a 'fatherhood bonus' for men.
writeBullets: Human capital theory — women invest less due to anticipated career breaks; CRITICISED because women now more educated than men
Radical feminist — patriarchy maintains glass ceiling through male networks and workplace culture
Walby (1994) — patriarchy operates across 6 structures: work, housework, sexuality, culture, violence, state
Marxist feminist — women's unpaid domestic labour serves capitalism; women as reserve army of labour (Braverman)
Liberal feminist — legal reform (Sex Discrimination Act 1975, Equality Act 2010) helps but gaps persist
Motherhood penalty — childcare costs force women to cut hours; fatherhood bonus — men's pay rises after children

keyTerms.def: A system of male dominance operating across social institutions — employment, family, politics, culture
Sociologist (1994) who identified six structures of patriarchy in her book 'Theorising Patriarchy'
Braverman's Marxist concept — women drawn into work when needed by capitalism, pushed out when not
The negative effect on women's careers and earnings after having children
The idea that earnings differences reflect differences in investment in education and skills

keyPoints.memTip: Walby's 6 structures: W-H-S-C-V-S — Work, Housework, Sexuality, Culture, Violence, State.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The motherhood penalty vs fatherhood bonus contrast is a strong piece of evidence — ensure students can use it precisely.

keyPoints.heading: Gender and Politics
keyPoints.content: Women won the right to vote in 1918 (over 30, and meeting a property qualification) and on equal terms with men in 1928. Political representation has improved: women now make up 35% of House of Commons MPs (2023), up from just 4% in 1979 — yet women are still 51% of the population, making Parliament significantly unrepresentative. Rwanda leads the world at 61% female parliamentarians, achieved partly through gender quotas. Sylvia Walby (1994) argued that patriarchal structures are embedded in politics as in all other institutions. Clare Short pushed for all-women shortlists in the Labour Party, which contributed to significant increases in female MPs. Beyond Parliament, women remain underrepresented in: the senior civil service, the judiciary, media ownership, corporate boards and religious leadership. The pattern holds internationally — only a minority of world governments are led by women.
writeBullets: Women's suffrage — 1918 (over 30, property qualification); 1928 (equal terms)
House of Commons — 35% female MPs (2023), up from 4% in 1979
Women = 51% of population, so Parliament remains unrepresentative
Rwanda — 61% female parliamentarians via gender quotas
Walby (1994) — patriarchal structures embedded in politics, law, media and religion
Women underrepresented in: senior civil service, judiciary, media ownership, corporate boards

keyTerms.def: The right to vote in political elections — women in the UK gained this fully in 1928
A mechanism used by UK political parties to increase the number of female candidates
Policies that require a minimum proportion of women in political or leadership positions

keyPoints.memTip: 1918 = some women vote; 1928 = equal vote. Up from 4% to 35% MPs — progress, but not parity.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say women got the vote in 1918 without noting the property/age restrictions — the distinction matters for accuracy.

keyPoints.heading: Intersectionality
keyPoints.content: Intersectionality, a term coined by legal scholar Kimberlé Crenshaw (1989), describes how different systems of oppression — class, gender, race, sexuality, disability, age — intersect and compound each other. You cannot understand the disadvantages faced by a Black working-class woman by simply adding up the disadvantages of being Black, being working class, and being a woman — these identities interact in ways that create unique, compounded experiences of discrimination. Patricia Hill Collins developed the concept of the 'matrix of domination' to describe how these overlapping systems work together. Applied to gender: 'women' is not a homogenous category. A wealthy white woman and a poor Black woman both face gender discrimination, but in very different ways and to very different degrees. Mainstream (second-wave) feminism has been criticised for centring the experiences of white, middle-class women and ignoring the additional dimensions of race, class and sexuality that shape other women's lives.
writeBullets: Intersectionality (Crenshaw, 1989) — systems of oppression intersect and compound each other
You cannot understand gender inequality in isolation from class, race, sexuality, disability
A Black working-class woman faces compounded disadvantages not reducible to any single dimension
Patricia Hill Collins — 'matrix of domination': overlapping systems of oppression
Mainstream feminism criticised for centring white, middle-class women's experiences
Intersectionality shows 'women' is not a uniform group — experiences vary enormously

keyTerms.def: Crenshaw's (1989) concept that systems of oppression (class, gender, race, etc.) intersect and compound each other
American legal scholar who coined 'intersectionality' in 1989 to describe compounded systems of discrimination
Patricia Hill Collins' term for the overlapping and interacting systems of oppression based on race, class, gender and sexuality

keyPoints.memTip: Intersectionality = the crossroads. Multiple roads of disadvantage meet at one point — one person, multiple oppressions.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This concept unlocks the top marks on evaluation questions — ensure students can apply it to any topic, not just gender.

tables.title: Gender Inequality: Key Areas, Evidence and Explanations
rows: Horizontal and vertical segregation; motherhood penalty
Nursing 89% female; construction 87% male (horizontal); 33% FTSE 100 directors female (vertical)
Patriarchy channels women into lower-paid sectors; glass ceiling blocks progression
35% female MPs (2023); 4% in 1979; Rwanda 61% via quotas
Girls outperform boys at GCSE and A-level; gender gap in STEM subjects
Liberal feminist: education reform works; BUT pay gap persists despite qualifications
Black women 4× more likely to die in childbirth than white women (MBRRACE-UK, 2021)
Gender cannot be understood separately from race and class

0.question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical occupational segregation?
options: Horizontal = men and women in different types of jobs; vertical = women underrepresented at senior levels
Horizontal = women blocked from promotion; vertical = men and women in different sectors
Horizontal = women earning less than men; vertical = women doing more unpaid work
Horizontal = the glass ceiling; vertical = the sticky floor
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Horizontal segregation is about which jobs men and women do; vertical is about how high they can rise within those jobs.
0.wrongFeedback: The terms are often confused. Horizontal = different job types (nursing vs construction). Vertical = different levels within the same organisation — the glass ceiling is a form of vertical segregation.

2.question: What does Kimberlé Crenshaw's concept of intersectionality argue?
options: Systems of oppression such as class, gender and race intersect and compound each other
Women face greater discrimination than any other group in society
Gender inequality can be solved by reforming legal institutions
Race is more important than gender in explaining inequality
2.correctFeedback: Correct. Crenshaw (1989) argued that oppressions based on gender, race, class and other factors interact — a Black working-class woman faces compounded disadvantages that cannot be understood by examining any dimension alone.
2.wrongFeedback: Intersectionality doesn't rank oppressions against each other. Crenshaw's point is that they intersect and compound — you can't understand one without the others.

commonMistakes: Confusing horizontal and vertical segregation — horizontal = different jobs, vertical = different levels within a job.
Saying the gender pay gap is 'illegal' — unequal pay for identical work is illegal, but the pay gap itself reflects different types of jobs and hours, which is harder to legislate away.
Treating all feminist perspectives as the same — liberal, radical, Marxist and Black feminist approaches give very different explanations and solutions.
Ignoring intersectionality — saying 'women' experience X without noting that class, race and other factors produce very different experiences.
Saying women got full voting rights in 1918 — the 1918 Act only covered women over 30 who met a property qualification; full equal suffrage came in 1928.
examTips: Quote specific statistics: '7.7% full-time gender pay gap' or '35% of MPs are women' — these signal that you actually know the subject.
For 'evaluate' questions, contrast feminist perspectives: e.g., liberal feminism (reform works) vs radical feminism (patriarchy is structural and reform is insufficient).
Use intersectionality as your 'wildcard' evaluation point — it works on almost any stratification question to show nuanced thinking.
On 'explain two reasons' questions, try to pick reasons from different levels: individual (human capital) and structural (patriarchy/childcare costs).
revisionCardBullets: Gender pay gap — 7.7% full-time; 14.9% including part-time (ONS, 2023)
Lifetime gap — women earn ~£300,000 less over a working life
Horizontal segregation — men and women in different job types (nursing vs construction)
Vertical segregation — women underrepresented at senior levels; glass ceiling
Only 33% of FTSE 100 directors female (2022); 35% of MPs female (2023)
Sticky floor — women stuck in lowest tiers; glass escalator (Williams) — men rise faster in female sectors
Walby (1994) — patriarchy operates across 6 structures: work, housework, sexuality, culture, violence, state
Marxist feminist — women's unpaid domestic labour serves capitalism; reserve army of labour
Motherhood penalty — career/pay costs for women after children; fatherhood bonus for men
Intersectionality (Crenshaw, 1989) — gender, race, class intersect and compound
Matrix of domination (Patricia Hill Collins) — overlapping systems of oppression
